# Chapter 1: The Finest Cartographer

<!-- scene:act1-sc01 -->

Dorren Hayle pressed the brass calipers against the vellum and held her breath, measuring the distance between the river fork and the ridgeline for the third time that morning, because in the Pressure Cartography Office there was no tolerance for approximation — every line committed to the master survey carried the weight of law, of tax borders and timber rights and the quiet fates of villages that existed only so long as the ink said they did. The office hummed around her: the scratch of nibs, the muffled clank of the great lithographic press two floors below, the occasional murmur of a junior draughtsman requesting a second opinion on contour intervals. She had worked here for nineteen years, rising from apprentice colorist to Senior Cartographer First Class, and in all that time she had never once submitted a map that required correction — a record the Director liked to mention at administrative luncheons, always with the same phrase: "our finest cartographer." It was into this precise, measured morning that the assignment arrived, carried by a clerk whose hands trembled slightly as he set the sealed envelope on her desk, the red wax stamped not with the office's familiar compass rose but with the Crown's own cartouche, and Dorren knew before she broke the seal that whatever lay inside would ask her to map something the office would rather leave blank.

***

<!-- scene:act1-sc02 -->

Dorren sat at her desk with the overlay sheets spread across every surface. The village — Tarren's Hollow, population four hundred and twelve at last census — should have been exactly here.

She tapped the empty space on the map. Blank parchment. Not even a contour line.

"It was here last quarter," she murmured, pulling the older survey. There. Tarren's Hollow. Roads, wells, the little temple marked with its standard sigil. Four hundred and twelve people going about their lives.

She returned to the new map. Nothing.
